records.ety: [F., fr. (assumed) L. rapagium, rapaticum, fr. rapere to carry off by force, to ravish. See Rapacious, Ravish.]

defs: Desolation by violence; violent ruin or destruction; devastation; havoc; waste; as, the ravage of a lion; the ravages of fire or tempest; the ravages of an army, or of time.
quotes: Would one think 't were possible for love To make such ravage in a noble soul?

records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Ravaged (rv"jd); p. pr. & vb. n. Ravaging (rv"*jng).]

defs: To lay waste by force; to desolate by violence; to commit havoc or devastation upon; to spoil; to plunder; to consume.
quotes: Already Caesar Has ravaged more than half the globe.
His lands were daily ravaged, his cattle driven away.
